draft.introduction: Mark 2 presents a series of controversy stories: the paralytic healed and forgiven, the call of Levi, disputes over fasting, and the Son of Man as lord of the Sabbath.

translation.text: And when he returned to Capernaum after some days, it was reported that he was at home. And many were gathered together, so that there was no more room. And they came, bringing to him a paralytic carried by four men. And when they could not get near him, they removed the roof above him. And when Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, “Son, your sins are forgiven.”

One Sabbath he was going through the grainfields, and his disciples began to pluck heads of grain. And the Pharisees were saying to him, “Look, why are they doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath?” And he said to them, “The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is lord even of the Sabbath.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο αὐτὸν ἐν τοῖς σάββασιν παραπορεύεσθαι

translator_notes.note: The claim to forgive sins provokes the charge of blasphemy because in Jewish theology only God forgives sin. Mark’s Jesus does not deny the theological premise; he validates it by healing, implying that the authority to forgive and the authority to heal share a single divine source.
